core_meaning: Most countries require definite article; gender varies (le Japon, la France)
primary_communicative_fn: Correctly naming and referring to countries in context
when_to_use: When discussing countries, their characteristics, or location-based information
pattern: le/la/les + nom de pays (most countries require article)
essential_vocabulary_slots: le, la, les, Japon, Brésil, France, Belgique, Argentine
common_learner_mistakes: Using 'le' for feminine countries (la France not le France)
primary_function: Proper reference to country entities with correct gender/article
key_forms: le Japon / la France / les États-Unis / la Belgique
essential_vocabulary: le, la, les, Japon, Brésil, France, Belgique, Argentina, Canada, Suisse
why_it_matters: Country article usage differs from English; gender agreement requires memorization
common_mistakes: Inconsistent gender application; omitting articles in constructions requiring them

core_meaning: Rare exception: some countries used without articles after prepositions
primary_communicative_fn: Understanding rare but valid prepositional omissions
when_to_use: After prepositions like 'en'; specific cases like 'à Cuba'
pattern: ∅ + nom de pays (en + fem. country / à + island countries)
key_variations: en Suisse (not 'en la Suisse') — after en with feminine
à Cuba / à Madagascar (island countries with à)
essential_vocabulary_slots: en, à, Suisse, France, Cuba, Madagascar, Chypre, Malte
common_learner_mistakes: Adding articles after 'en' (→ en la France is wrong)
Confusing prepositional omission rules with default article requirement
primary_function: Correct prepositional phrases with country names
key_forms: en Suisse / en France / à Cuba / à Madagascar
essential_vocabulary: en, à, Suisse, France, Cuba, Madagascar, voyage, habiter
why_it_matters: Prepositional usage differs from English; 'en' + feminine country is standard French
common_mistakes: Adding article after prepositions; overusing articles where omitted

structure_label: Contractions de + articles (du, de la, des)
core_meaning: Preposition 'de' contracts with articles: de+le=du, de+la=de la, de+les=des
primary_communicative_fn: Correctly forming possessive/origin phrases with contracted prepositions
when_to_use: When expressing origin, possession, or composition with article nouns
pattern: de + le = du / de + la = de la / de + les = des
key_variations: de France (from France) — no contraction with definite article
de la Belgique (of Belgium — preposition + article combination)
essential_vocabulary_slots: du, de, la, des, Japon, France, Belgique, États-Unis, venir, être
primary_function: Fluent expression of origin, possession, and composition
key_forms: du Japon / de la Belgique / des États-Unis / de France
essential_vocabulary: du, de, la, des, Japon, France, venir, être, habiter, pays
why_it_matters: Contraction is mandatory; failure to contract signals non-native speech
common_mistakes: Saying 'de le Japon' instead of 'du Japon'; forgetting plural 'des'

core_meaning: Indicates unspecified quantity of uncountable or mass nouns
primary_communicative_fn: Talking about quantities of things that cannot be counted individually
when_to_use: When discussing food, liquids, abstract nouns, or any uncountable quantity
essential_vocabulary_slots: du, de la, de l', pain, viande, eau, crème, courage, temps
common_learner_mistakes: Omitting partitive in favor of definite article
chunk_compatibility: Food/drink chunks (*du pain*, *de l'eau*, *de la patience*)
primary_function: Expressing indeterminate quantity of uncountable nouns
key_forms: du pain / de la viande / de l'eau / de la crème
essential_vocabulary: du, de la, de l', pain, viande, eau, crème, courage, patience, liberté
why_it_matters: Partitive is foundational to French indefinite quantity; essential for food/drink conversations
common_mistakes: Using 'un/une' with uncountable; omitting article; using definite instead

core_meaning: Indicates unspecified singular (un/une) or plural (des) countable nouns
primary_communicative_fn: Introducing or referring to countable nouns non-specifically
when_to_use: With countable nouns when identity is indefinite or new
essential_vocabulary_slots: un, une, des, livre, table, ami, enfant, personne, chat
chunk_compatibility: Introduction/identification chunks (*C'est un livre*, *J'ai un ami*)
key_forms: un livre / une table / des amis / un enfant
essential_vocabulary: un, une, des, livre, table, ami, enfant, personne, chat, maison
why_it_matters: Indefinite articles are foundational; omission is ungrammatical in French
common_mistakes: Omitting article entirely; confusing gender; misplacing in sentences

core_meaning: Indicates specific, known, or previously mentioned nouns
primary_communicative_fn: Referring to specific definite entities with established identity
when_to_use: With specific known nouns; general statements; unique entities
essential_vocabulary_slots: le, la, les, chat, maison, enfant, livre, jour, soleil
common_learner_mistakes: Using definite article for general statements (partly correct in French)
Omitting article with unique nouns (le soleil, la lune)
chunk_compatibility: Definite reference chunks (*Je vois le chat*, *La maison est grande*)
key_forms: le chat / la maison / les enfants / le soleil
essential_vocabulary: le, la, les, chat, maison, enfant, livre, jour, soleil, terre
why_it_matters: Definite articles are mandatory before known nouns; omission is ungrammatical
common_mistakes: Omitting with unique nouns; confusing gender; inconsistent application

core_meaning: Basic sentence negation with two-part structure
primary_communicative_fn: Denying statements or creating negative assertions
when_to_use: To negate verbs; transforms affirmative to negative
key_variations: Je ne sais pas (I don't know)
Il ne vient pas (He's not coming)
Je ne veux pas (I don't want)
essential_vocabulary_slots: ne, pas, sais, viens, avoir, veux, aller, faire
common_learner_mistakes: Forgetting 'ne' in formal speech (though common in informal)
Omitting 'ne' entirely (→ ungrammatical in formal French)
chunk_compatibility: Negation chunks (*Je ne sais pas*, *Pas possible*)
key_forms: Je ne sais pas / Il ne vient pas / Nous n'avons pas
essential_vocabulary: ne, pas, sais, viens, avoir, veux, aller, faire, pouvoir
why_it_matters: Negation is foundational to communicating refusal/denial; omission changes meaning
common_mistakes: Omitting 'ne' in formal contexts; misplacing 'pas'; doubling negation incorrectly

core_meaning: Indicates possession by first person singular; agrees with noun gender/number
primary_communicative_fn: Expressing possession or relationship for first person
when_to_use: When indicating what belongs to or is associated with the speaker
key_variations: mon école (my school — masculine, elision with vowel)
essential_vocabulary_slots: mon, ma, mes, père, mère, frère, école, ami, sœur
common_learner_mistakes: Using 'mon' with feminine nouns starting with consonant
Not using 'mon' with feminine nouns starting with vowel (→ mon amie, not ma amie)
primary_function: First-person possession; family and relationship expression
key_forms: mon père / ma mère / mes frères / mon école
essential_vocabulary: mon, ma, mes, père, mère, frère, sœur, école, ami, maison
why_it_matters: Possessive agreement with noun gender is mandatory; errors are very noticeable
common_mistakes: Using feminine possessive with masculine noun; forgetting elision with vowels

core_meaning: Indicates possession by second person singular; agrees with noun gender/number
primary_communicative_fn: Asking about or referring to what belongs to the listener
when_to_use: When asking about or referring to what the listener possesses
key_variations: ton école (your school — masculine with vowel)
essential_vocabulary_slots: ton, ta, tes, livre, sœur, ami, école, maison, voiture
common_learner_mistakes: Not agreeing with possessor gender (it agrees with noun gender)
Forgetting elision with vowels (ton, not ta)
primary_function: Second-person possession in questions and statements
key_forms: ton livre / ta sœur / tes amis / ton école
essential_vocabulary: ton, ta, tes, livre, sœur, ami, école, maison, voiture, parent
why_it_matters: Possessive agreement is mandatory and very noticeable when incorrect
common_mistakes: Gender confusion; omitting elision; wrong possessive form

core_meaning: Indicates possession by third person singular; agrees with noun gender/number
primary_communicative_fn: Referring to what belongs to or is associated with another person/thing
when_to_use: When discussing what a third person or thing possesses or has
key_variations: son amie (his/her female friend — feminine with vowel)
essential_vocabulary_slots: son, sa, ses, chien, voiture, enfant, ami, école, maison
common_learner_mistakes: Confusing possessive adjective with reflexive pronoun (se)
Using feminine possessive with masculine noun
Forgetting that son/sa/ses indicate relationship, not gender of possessor
chunk_compatibility: Third-person reference chunks (*Son frère*, *Sa maison*, *Ses enfants*)
primary_function: Third-person possession; indirect reference to relationships
key_forms: son chien / sa voiture / ses enfants / son amie
essential_vocabulary: son, sa, ses, chien, voiture, enfant, ami, école, maison, frère
why_it_matters: Possessive agreement with noun is mandatory; confusion affects comprehension
common_mistakes: Using wrong possessive for noun gender; confusing with reflexive pronouns

core_meaning: Points to or indicates specific nouns; agrees with gender and number
primary_communicative_fn: Singling out specific items or entities for identification or emphasis
when_to_use: When pointing out or emphasizing specific nouns in space or discourse
pattern: ce (masc) / cet (masc before vowel) / cette (fem) / ces (plural) + nom
key_variations: cet homme (this/that man — masculine with vowel)
essential_vocabulary_slots: ce, cet, cette, ces, livre, fille, homme, maison, jour, arbre
primary_function: Demonstrative reference; pointing out specific entities
key_forms: ce livre / cette fille / cet homme / ces maisons
essential_vocabulary: ce, cet, cette, ces, livre, fille, homme, maison, jour, arbre
why_it_matters: Demonstratives are foundational for discourse cohesion; gender agreement is mandatory
common_mistakes: Using 'cet' with feminine; missing liaisons; gender confusion

core_meaning: Indicates entirety, each, or all entities; full gender/number agreement
when_to_use: When referring to entire categories, all items, or each individual
key_variations: tout le monde (everyone — literally 'all the world')
tous les jours (every day / all days)
essential_vocabulary_slots: tout, toute, tous, toutes, monde, journée, jour, fille, monde
common_learner_mistakes: Not agreeing in gender and number
chunk_compatibility: Totality chunks (*tout le monde*, *tous les jours*, *toute la nuit*)
key_forms: tout le monde / toute la journée / tous les jours / toutes les filles
essential_vocabulary: tout, toute, tous, toutes, monde, journée, jour, fille, moment, semaine
why_it_matters: Indefinite adjectives are common in French; agreement errors are noticeable
common_mistakes: Gender/number disagreement; omitting required article; using wrong form

core_meaning: Introduces relative clauses; qui=subject, que=object, où=location
primary_communicative_fn: Joining clauses to add information about nouns
when_to_use: When adding descriptive or identifying information about previously mentioned nouns
key_variations: l'homme qui parle (the man who speaks — qui as subject)
le livre que je lis (the book that I read — que as object)
la ville où j'habite (the city where I live — où for location)
la personne à qui je parle (the person to whom I speak — qui after preposition)
essential_vocabulary_slots: qui, que, où, homme, livre, ville, parler, lire, habiter
common_learner_mistakes: Using 'que' for subject (→ must use qui)
Omitting relative pronoun (→ required in French)
chunk_compatibility: Descriptive chunks (*L'homme qui parle*, *Le livre que j'aime*)
primary_function: Relative clause formation; adding detail to nouns
essential_vocabulary: qui, que, où, homme, livre, ville, personne, parler, lire, habiter
why_it_matters: Relative pronouns are essential for complex sentence formation
common_mistakes: Misusing qui/que; omitting relative pronoun; wrong preposition with lequel

core_meaning: Replaces 'de + noun' in relative clauses; avoids repetition
primary_communicative_fn: Forming relative clauses with possessive or 'of' relationships
when_to_use: When the relative clause needs 'de' relationship (possession, need, speaking about)
key_variations: la personne dont je parle (the person about whom I speak)
le livre dont j'ai besoin (the book of which I have need)
la fille dont le père... (the girl whose father... / the girl of whom the father...)
les choses dont on discute (the things of which we discuss)
essential_vocabulary_slots: dont, personne, livre, parler, besoin, père, mère, discuter
chunk_compatibility: Complex relative clauses (*Le livre dont j'ai besoin*, *La personne dont je parle*)
key_forms: dont je parle / dont j'ai besoin / dont le père
essential_vocabulary: dont, personne, livre, parler, besoin, père, mère, fille, discuter
why_it_matters: Dont is essential for avoiding repetition and creating fluid relative clauses
common_mistakes: Using 'de + qui' instead of dont; omitting dont; misplacing in clause

core_meaning: Pronouns replacing prepositional phrases (y) and quantities/origins (en)
primary_communicative_fn: Avoiding repetition of locations and quantities in discourse
when_to_use: When referring back to previously mentioned places (y) or quantities/origins (en)
pattern: sujet + verbe + y/en / position before conjugated verb
key_variations: J'y vais (I'm going there — replaces à + location)
J'en viens (I'm coming from there — replaces de + location)
J'en veux trois (I want three of them — replaces de + noun)
Vous en avez? (Do you have some? — replaces de + noun)
essential_vocabulary_slots: y, en, aller, venir, avoir, vouloir, besoin, trois
common_learner_mistakes: Using both y and en together incorrectly
Misplacing y/en in relation to verb
Confusion about which pronoun replaces which construction
chunk_compatibility: Pronoun substitution chunks (*J'y vais*, *J'en veux*, *On en parle*)
primary_function: Efficient pronoun substitution; avoiding repetition in discourse
key_forms: J'y vais / J'en viens / J'en veux / Vous en avez?
essential_vocabulary: y, en, aller, venir, avoir, vouloir, besoin, parler, penser
why_it_matters: Y/en are frequent in natural French; mastery essential for fluent conversation
common_mistakes: Misplacing pronouns; using both y and en; forgetting ne in negative structures
